flashcards.definition: A unit of angle measure where a full rotation equals 360°.
A unit of angle measure where the angle is subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius; a full rotation = 2π radians.
Multiply the degree measure by π/180.
Multiply the radian measure by 180/π.
An angle with its vertex at the origin and initial side along the positive x-axis.
s = rθ, where s is the arc length, r is the radius, and θ is the central angle in radians.
A = ½r²θ, where A is the sector area, r is the radius, and θ is the central angle in radians.
A 'pie-slice' region bounded by two radii and an arc of a circle.
An angle whose vertex is at the center of a circle and whose sides are radii.
A portion of the circumference of a circle.
sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse.
cos θ = adjacent / hypotenuse.
tan θ = opposite / adjacent.
Mnemonic: Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent.
The longest side of a right triangle, opposite the 90° angle.
An isosceles right triangle with side ratio 1 : 1 : √2.
A right triangle with side ratio 1 : √3 : 2 (short leg : long leg : hypotenuse).
A circle with radius 1 centered at the origin; points are (cos θ, sin θ).
Angles that share the same terminal side; found by adding or subtracting multiples of 360° (or 2π).
The acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis; always between 0° and 90°.
All (Q I), Sine (Q II), Tangent (Q III), Cosine (Q IV) — tells which trig functions are positive in each quadrant.
An angle measured clockwise from the positive x-axis.
The ray that rotates from the initial side to form an angle in standard position.
A = ½ab sin C, where a and b are two sides and C is the included angle.
For a regular n-gon inscribed in a circle of radius r: side = 2r sin(π/n).
A = ½nr² sin(2π/n) for a regular n-gon inscribed in a circle of radius r.
d = √(l² + w²); the angle with the base can be found using tan θ = w/l.
A = ½r²θ (θ in radians); links radian measure to geometric area.
The y-coordinate of the point where the terminal side meets the unit circle.
The x-coordinate of the point where the terminal side meets the unit circle.
sin θ / cos θ; undefined when cos θ = 0.
[−1, 1]; both functions output values between −1 and 1.
All real numbers except π/2 + nπ (where cos θ = 0).
csc θ = 1/sin θ; undefined when sin θ = 0.
sec θ = 1/cos θ; undefined when cos θ = 0.
cot θ = cos θ/sin θ = 1/tan θ; undefined when sin θ = 0.
(−∞, −1] ∪ [1, ∞); they never have values between −1 and 1.
All real numbers except nπ (where sin θ = 0).
The length of one full cycle; for y = sin x and y = cos x, period = 2π.
Half the distance between max and min values; for basic sin/cos, amplitude = 1.
The horizontal line halfway between max and min; for basic sin/cos, midline = y = 0.
Having the shape of a sine or cosine wave.
A horizontal translation; cos x = sin(x + π/2) is sine shifted left by π/2.

u2_l2.4.title: Graphs of Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, Cosecant

flashcards.definition: π — tangent and cotangent repeat every π radians.
Vertical asymptotes at x = π/2 + nπ (where cos x = 0).
Vertical asymptotes at x = nπ (where sin x = 0).
(−∞, −1] ∪ [1, ∞); no values between −1 and 1.
An increasing S-curve between asymptotes, passing through the origin in each period.

u2_l2.5.title: Transformations of Trig Graphs (amplitude, period, phase shift)

flashcards.definition: |A| in y = A sin(Bx − C) + D; the maximum displacement from the midline.
2π/|B|; the horizontal length of one complete cycle.
C/B; the horizontal shift of the graph (right if positive).
Moves the midline from y = 0 to y = D.
If A < 0, the graph is flipped over the x-axis.
f(−x) = f(x); symmetric about the y-axis. Cosine is even.
f(−x) = −f(x); symmetric about the origin. Sine and tangent are odd.
The property that a function repeats at regular intervals: f(x + P) = f(x).
2π — the smallest interval after which the function repeats.
π — tangent and cotangent repeat every π radians.
Number of complete cycles per second; measured in hertz (Hz).
The maximum pressure variation; corresponds to loudness or volume.
T = 1/f; the time for one complete vibration cycle.
Unit of frequency: 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.
Periodic loudness fluctuations caused by two overlapping waves of slightly different frequencies.

u3_l3.1.title: Definition & Domains of Inverse Trig Functions

flashcards.definition: Inverse sine; domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2].
Inverse cosine; domain [−1, 1], range [0, π].
Inverse tangent; domain (−∞, ∞), range (−π/2, π/2).
The unique output of an inverse trig function within its restricted range.
Limiting the domain of a trig function so it becomes one-to-one and thus invertible.
S-shaped, increasing from (−1, −π/2) to (1, π/2), passing through the origin.
Decreasing curve from (−1, π) to (1, 0).
Horizontal asymptotes at y = −π/2 (left) and y = π/2 (right).
The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f over the line y = x.
Increasing S-curve from bottom-left to top-right, always between y = −π/2 and y = π/2.
The first solution returned by an inverse trig function within its restricted range.
If sin θ = a, then θ = arcsin(a) + 2nπ or θ = π − arcsin(a) + 2nπ.
If cos θ = a, then θ = ±arccos(a) + 2nπ.
If tan θ = a, then θ = arctan(a) + nπ.
Rearrange the equation so the trig function is alone on one side before applying the inverse.
The angle above the horizontal line of sight, found with arctan(height/distance).
The angle below the horizontal line of sight to an object below the observer.
θ = arccos(u·v / (|u||v|)) gives the angle between two vectors.
n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; solve for θ₂ using arcsin.
θ = arctan(v_y / v_x) for a projectile's initial velocity components.
= x, for all x in [−1, 1]. The 'round trip' starting from a value.
= θ only if θ ∈ [−π/2, π/2]. Otherwise, the principal value is returned.
The unique angle in the restricted range that yields the given trig value.
arcsin(sin θ) ≠ θ when θ is outside [−π/2, π/2]; you must find the equivalent angle in range.
arctan(Im/Re); the angle of a complex number or signal in the complex plane.
θ = arccos(P/S); relates real power to apparent power in AC circuits.
θ = arctan(reactance / resistance); describes the phase between voltage and current.
Using arcsin to calculate steering angles for phased antenna arrays.
Using inverse trig to determine joint angles in mechanical linkages from known lengths.
The fundamental Pythagorean identity, true for all θ.
Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by cos²θ.
Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by sin²θ.
On the unit circle, x = cos θ, y = sin θ, and x² + y² = 1 gives sin² + cos² = 1.
Replace one trig function with another to simplify: e.g., sin²θ = 1 − cos²θ.
Cosecant is the reciprocal of sine.
Secant is the reciprocal of cosine.
Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent.
Two functions f and g such that f(θ) · g(θ) = 1 (e.g., sin · csc = 1).
Rewrite csc, sec, cot in terms of sin and cos, then simplify.

flashcards.term: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ
flashcards.definition: The quotient identity for tangent.

flashcards.term: cot θ = cos θ / sin θ
flashcards.definition: The quotient identity for cotangent.
Convert tan or cot into sin/cos to simplify or verify expressions.
Quotient + Pythagorean + reciprocal identities work together for simplification.
Since tan = sin/cos and cot = cos/sin, their product is 1.
A pair of trig functions whose values swap at complementary angles: sin/cos, tan/cot, sec/csc.
Two angles that sum to 90° (or π/2 radians).
The sine of the complement equals the cosine.
The tangent of the complement equals the cotangent.
The secant of the complement equals the cosecant.
Showing that one side of the equation can be transformed into the other using known identities.
Start with the more complicated side — it's easier to simplify than to build up.
Rewriting all trig functions as sin and cos often reveals simplifications.
Multiply by (1 + sin θ)/(1 + sin θ) or similar to create difference-of-squares patterns.
Identities are proven by transforming one side; treating both sides equally assumes the result.
Replace csc, sec, tan, cot with sin and cos expressions.
Use sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 or variants to reduce terms.
Factor common terms or get a common denominator for fractions.
Cancel common factors in numerator and denominator.
Reach a simpler equivalent expression — fewer terms, simpler functions.
A sin(ωt) + B cos(ωt) = R sin(ωt + φ) with R = √(A²+B²).
R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g; uses double-angle identity.
Double-angle identity; shows max at θ = 45° for projectile range.
x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); expanded using sum identity for analysis.
φ = arctan(B/A) when combining A sin + B cos into a single sinusoid.

u5_l5.1.title: Sum & Difference Formulas for Sine & Cosine

flashcards.definition: sin A cos B + cos A sin B.
sin A cos B − cos A sin B.
cos A cos B − sin A sin B.
cos A cos B + sin A sin B.
Sum/difference formulas express trig of non-standard angles in terms of known special angles.
2 sin A cos A.
cos²A − sin²A.
2 cos²A − 1.
1 − 2 sin²A.
2 tan A / (1 − tan²A).
±√((1 − cos A)/2); sign determined by the quadrant of A/2.
±√((1 + cos A)/2); sign determined by the quadrant of A/2.
sin A / (1 + cos A).
(1 − cos A) / sin A.
Determine which quadrant A/2 is in to decide + or −.
sin A cos B = ½[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)].
cos A cos B = ½[cos(A−B) + cos(A+B)].
sin A + sin B = 2 sin((A+B)/2) cos((A−B)/2).
cos A + cos B = 2 cos((A+B)/2) cos((A−B)/2).
Product-to-sum simplifies integration; sum-to-product helps solve equations.
sin²x = (1 − cos 2x)/2.
cos²x = (1 + cos 2x)/2.
= sin(2x)/2 by the double-angle formula.
Reduce powers, convert products to sums, or consolidate into simpler forms.
Choose the identity that eliminates the most complex part of the expression.
P(t) = (V₀I₀/2)[cosφ − cos(2ωt+φ)]; constant part = average power.
P_avg = V₀I₀ cosφ / 2; the oscillating term averages to zero.
Sum-to-product: sinω₁t + sinω₂t = 2cos(Δωt/2)sin(ω_avg t).
Carrier × signal uses product-to-sum to produce sum and difference frequencies.
Decomposing signals into sine and cosine components using these identities.
Use sin²x = (1−cos2x)/2, then integrate: x/2 − sin(2x)/4 + C.
Product-to-sum converts to integrable sums of sin/cos.
= 2 cos 2x by the chain rule.
= 1 as x → 0; fundamental limit in calculus.
In integrals like ∫√(1−x²)dx, let x = sinθ; uses sin²+cos²=1.
Rearrange so sin θ = k, cos θ = k, or tan θ = k.
The first answer from the inverse trig function.
Determine which quadrants yield the correct sign to find all solutions.
Add full-period multiples (2nπ or nπ) to cover all solutions.
Equation with trig function raised to the first power, e.g., 2cosθ + 1 = 0.
Let u = sinθ (or cosθ) to convert to au²+bu+c = 0.
Factor the quadratic, then set each factor to zero.
u = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / 2a when factoring is difficult.
Discard u values outside [−1,1] for sin/cos.
Replace u with the trig function and solve for θ.
An equation where the argument of the trig function is nθ (e.g., 2θ, 3θ).
If θ ∈ [0, 2π) and argument is nθ, solve for nθ ∈ [0, 2nπ).
Simplifies the equation to a standard form in u.
After finding all u values, θ = u/n gives the final answers.
Multiple-angle equations typically have more solutions than single-angle ones in a given interval.
θ = arcsin(k) + 2nπ or θ = π − arcsin(k) + 2nπ, n ∈ ℤ.
θ = ±arccos(k) + 2nπ, n ∈ ℤ.
θ = arctan(k) + nπ, n ∈ ℤ (period π).
n is any integer: …, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, …
When the domain is all reals, there are infinitely many solutions — general form captures them all.
y = A sin(Bt − C) + D or y = A cos(Bt − C) + D for periodic phenomena.
(max − min)/2; the intensity of variation.
B = 2π/period; controls how fast the cycle repeats.
(max + min)/2; the average value.
Horizontal shift aligning the model with data.
x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); solving for t when x = x₀ requires inverse trig.
Occurs when the driving frequency equals the natural frequency, maximizing amplitude.
Points where displacement is always zero: sin(nπx/L) = 0 → x = kL/n.
ω = 2πf; relates to the period by T = 2π/ω.
Determines the initial position of the oscillator at t = 0.
Get the trig function alone before applying inverse.
Don't miss second solutions from quadrant symmetry.
If the equation has mixed trig functions, convert using identities.
Expand the interval proportionally, find all solutions, then divide.
Plug solutions back in to catch errors.
a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C for any triangle.
Cases where the Law of Sines is directly applicable.
A triangle with no right angle.
Finding all unknown sides and angles.
C = 180° − A − B; always find the missing angle first.
SSA configuration; may yield 0, 1, or 2 valid triangles.
sin B > 1 → the given side is too short to form a triangle.
sin B = 1 (right triangle) or the supplement B' makes A + B' ≥ 180°.
Both B and B' = 180° − B produce valid triangles (A + B' < 180°).
h = b sin A. If a < h → 0 triangles; a = h → 1; h < a < b → 2; a ≥ b → 1.
c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C; generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.
Two sides and the included angle → use Law of Cosines to find the third side.
Three sides → rearrange to find each angle: cos C = (a²+b²−c²)/(2ab).
When C = 90°, cos C = 0 and the Law of Cosines reduces to c² = a² + b².
Find the largest angle first (opposite the longest side) to determine if the triangle is obtuse.
Area = ½ ab sin C; efficient with SAS data.
Area = √(s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)) where s = (a+b+c)/2.
s = (a + b + c) / 2; used in Heron's formula.
When two sides and their included angle are known (SAS).
When all three sides are known (SSS).
Direction measured clockwise from north, e.g., N30°E or simply 030°.
A known distance between two observation points.
Using angles from two known points to determine the position of a third.
Course = intended path; heading = direction the craft points (may differ due to wind/current).
Straight-line distance and direction from start to end.
Forces along members are found by resolving force triangles using Laws of Sines/Cosines.
A closed loop of measured angles and distances; areas computed by breaking into triangles.
Rise over run; the angle is found using tan⁻¹(rise/run).
Breaking a force into components along and perpendicular to a direction.
Divide into triangles, compute each area (½ab sin C), and sum.
The apparent shift of a nearby star against distant stars as Earth orbits — used to find distance.
Distance at which a star has a parallax of 1 arcsecond; ≈ 3.26 light-years.
Mean Earth–Sun distance ≈ 1.496 × 10⁸ km; used as the baseline for parallax.
The angle between two objects as seen from an observer.
Historically calculated using triangulation from two points on Earth's surface.
The origin of the polar coordinate system.
The ray from the pole in the direction of the positive x-axis.
The directed distance from the pole to the point.
The angle measured counter-clockwise from the polar axis.
The same point has infinitely many polar representations: (r, θ+2nπ) and (−r, θ+π+2nπ).
Heart-shaped curve; r = a(1 ± cosθ) or r = a(1 ± sinθ).
r = a cos(nθ); has n petals (n odd) or 2n petals (n even).
r = a + b cos θ; shape depends on ratio a/b (with/without inner loop).
r = aθ; distance from origin increases linearly with angle.
Replace θ → −θ (x-axis sym), θ → π−θ (y-axis sym), r → −r (origin sym).
x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ.
r = √(x²+y²), θ = arctan(y/x) (with quadrant adjustment).
Links the radial distance to Cartesian coordinates.
Replace r², r cos θ, r sin θ with x²+y², x, y (or vice versa).
arctan gives values in (−π/2, π/2); add π for Q II/III points.
r = √(a²+b²); the distance from z to the origin in the complex plane.
The angle θ from the positive real axis to z.
z = r(cosθ + i sinθ) = r cis θ.
Multiply moduli and add arguments: z₁z₂ = r₁r₂ cis(θ₁+θ₂).
Divide moduli and subtract arguments: z₁/z₂ = (r₁/r₂) cis(θ₁−θ₂).
[r cis θ]ⁿ = rⁿ cis(nθ); powers of complex numbers in polar form.
Raise r to the n and multiply θ by n.
The n roots form a regular n-gon on a circle of radius r^(1/n).
1, cis(2π/3), cis(4π/3): three equally spaced points on the unit circle.
A complex number representing the amplitude and phase of a sinusoidal signal.
Z = R + jX; complex resistance combining resistance (R) and reactance (X).
Engineers use j instead of i (since i denotes current).
V = IZ; phasor voltage = phasor current × impedance.
θ = arctan(X/R); the angle between voltage and current.
Add complex exponentials instead of trig functions; the resultant's magnitude and phase follow directly.
A complex-valued function; its modulus squared |ψ|² gives probability density.
P(x) = |ψ(x)|²; probability from the modulus of a complex wave function.
Results from adding wave phasors: constructive when in phase, destructive when π out of phase.
e^(iθ) = cosθ + i sinθ; connects exponential and trig representations.
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, e.g., velocity, force.
A quantity with magnitude only, e.g., speed, temperature.
v = ⟨v₁, v₂⟩; the horizontal and vertical components.
|v| = √(v₁² + v₂²); the length of the vector.
A vector of magnitude 1; v̂ = v / |v|.
u + v = ⟨u₁+v₁, u₂+v₂⟩; tip-to-tail or parallelogram rule.
u − v = u + (−v) = ⟨u₁−v₁, u₂−v₂⟩.
cv = ⟨cv₁, cv₂⟩; changes magnitude (and direction if c < 0).
Place u and v tail-to-tail; the diagonal of the parallelogram is u + v.
|u + v| ≤ |u| + |v|; the sum's magnitude ≤ sum of magnitudes.
u · v = u₁v₁ + u₂v₂; a scalar result.
u · v = |u||v| cos θ; relates to the angle between vectors.
u ⊥ v ⟺ u · v = 0.
comp_u v = (v · u) / |u|; the signed length of v along u.
proj_u v = (v · u / |u|²) u; the vector component of v along u.
u × v; a vector perpendicular to both u and v (3D only).
u × v = −(v × u); reversing the order negates the result.
= |u||v| sin θ; the area of the parallelogram formed by u and v.
Curl fingers from u toward v; thumb points in direction of u × v.
u × v = 0⃗ iff u ∥ v (since sin 0° = 0).
Net force = 0⃗; the object is stationary or moving at constant velocity.
W = F · d; the dot product of force and displacement.
τ = r × F; the cross product of position vector and force.
Breaking a vector into perpendicular components along chosen axes.
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object.
Moving every point by adding a displacement vector.
[[cosθ, −sinθ], [sinθ, cosθ]]; rotates a vector by angle θ CCW.
A vector perpendicular to a surface; used for lighting calculations.
Brightness ∝ max(0, n̂ · L̂); dot product of surface normal and light direction.
Used for shading, reflections, visibility testing, and angle computations.
r(t) = ⟨x(t), y(t)⟩; maps a parameter t to a position vector.
r'(t) = ⟨x'(t), y'(t)⟩; tangent to the curve at time t.
|r'(t)| = √(x'² + y'²); the magnitude of velocity.
x = f(t), y = g(t) define a curve in terms of a parameter.
L = ∫ₐᵇ √(x'² + y'²) dt; total distance traveled along the curve.
An identity is true for ALL valid θ; an equation may be true only for specific θ.
Transform one side to match the other; never move terms across the equals sign.
Replace tan, sec, csc, cot with sin θ and cos θ expressions.
Multiply numerator & denominator by (1 + sinθ) or (1 − cosθ) to simplify.
Look for Pythagorean patterns, common factors, or difference of squares.
Replacing x with a trig expression to eliminate a radical.
Use x = a sinθ; the radical becomes a cosθ.
Use x = a tanθ; the radical becomes a secθ.
Use x = a secθ; the radical becomes a tanθ.
After substituting, draw a right triangle to convert back to x.
x = f(t), y = g(t); the curve is traced as t varies.
Solve one equation for t and substitute into the other.
The direction the curve is traced as t increases.
x = r cosθ, y = r sinθ traces a circle of radius r.
x = x₀ + at, y = y₀ + bt traces a line through (x₀, y₀) with direction ⟨a, b⟩.
The derivative of sine is cosine.
The derivative of cosine is negative sine.
Fundamental trigonometric limit (x in radians, x → 0).
Antiderivative of sine.
Used to integrate sin²x or cos²x: cos²x = (1+cos2x)/2.
Know exact values at 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° and all quadrants.
y = A sin(Bx − C) + D: identify amplitude |A|, period 2π/B, phase C/B, midline D.
Pythagorean, reciprocal, quotient, double-angle, sum/difference, power-reduction.
Isolate trig function → inverse → quadrant analysis → general solution if needed.
AP problems often combine multiple concepts in one question.
The foundation: exact values of sin, cos, tan at key angles.
Pythagorean, reciprocal, quotient, double-angle, sum/difference — the algebraic toolkit.
Isolate, use inverses, analyze quadrants, give general solutions.
Solve any triangle: AAS/ASA/SSA use Law of Sines; SAS/SSS use Law of Cosines.
Dot/cross products, polar form, De Moivre's Theorem, parametric equations.
